mnemonics.title: PEAS Helps Agents Succeed
mnemonics.content: PEAS = Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors. When designing an agent, first define these four components to know what success looks like, where the agent operates, what it can do, and what it can perceive.

mnemonics.title: Reflex Models Goals Utilities Learn
mnemonics.content: Types of agents in order of increasing intelligence: Simple Reflex → Model-based → Goal-based → Utility-based → Learning agent. Each adds more sophistication to handle complex environments.

mnemonics.title: A RIDE For Agent Properties
mnemonics.content: A RIDE = Accessible/Inaccessible, Random/Stochastic, Interactive/Sequential, Discrete/Continuous, Episodic/Static-Dynamic. These are environment properties that determine agent design.

mnemonics.title: Agent = Program + Architecture
mnemonics.content: Intelligent Agent = Agent Program + Agent Architecture. The program implements the agent function, the architecture provides the hardware/framework to execute it.

mnemonics.title: Rational Agent: Best Expected Action
mnemonics.content: A rational agent selects actions that maximize its expected performance measure, given the perceptual history and built-in knowledge. It's not omniscient—it's about doing the best with what it knows.

acronyms.usage: Use when analyzing or designing any agent. First define PEAS to understand the agent's task requirements.
Agent decision loop: Observe (sensors), Orient (process/interpret), Decide (agent function), Act (actuators).
Agent architecture components: Reflex (reactive), Intention (goal-directed), State (internal model), Evaluation (utility).

visualTips.visualization: Picture a loop: Sensors → Agent → Actuators → Environment → Sensors (again). Draw it as a circular flow with the environment surrounding the agent like a backdrop.
Think of a function machine: Input = Percept Sequence → Output = Action. The agent program is the internal mechanism, the agent function is the mathematical mapping.

Use a 2x2 grid matrix: Accessible vs Inaccessible (top row), Deterministic vs Stochastic (bottom row). Mark real-world examples in each quadrant (e.g., Chess = Accessible+Deterministic, Poker = Accessible+Stochastic).
Draw a pyramid: Base = Simple Reflex (smallest knowledge), Middle = Model-based & Goal-based, Top = Utility-based, Peak = Learning agent (most sophisticated).

analogies.analogy: A robot waiter in a restaurant. Sensors = eyes/ears to observe customers and food, Actuators = hands to serve, Agent Function = brain to decide what to do based on orders, Performance = customer satisfaction and speed.
A thermostat. It only reacts to current temperature (percept), not to history. If too hot → turn off AC. No memory, no planning.
A driver using a mental map. It maintains an internal state (how the world works) to navigate even when landmarks are temporarily out of sight.
A GPS navigator. It doesn't just react—it has a destination (goal) and plans a sequence of actions to reach it, considering future consequences.
A person choosing between job offers. It evaluates multiple goals (salary, work-life balance, growth) using a utility function to pick the best overall outcome.

Comparing video games: Chess = Fully Accessible, Deterministic, Discrete; Real-time strategy game = Partially Accessible, Stochastic, Continuous. The environment type determines how 'smart' the agent needs to be.
